paragraphs.text: I commend the Commission's work to address existing "shortcomings" in consumer law and support the goal of creating a fairer digital single market. To this end, I propose a comprehensive three-pillar framework designed to protect consumers across all major digital transaction models. Pillar 1: The Right to Perpetual Access for Direct Purchases (The Foundational Rule) This pillar addresses the core problem of consumers losing access to digital products they have purchased directly, which can result in significant "financial detriment". The Proposed Rule: Any discrete digital product sold directly to a consumer for a one-time payment must include a guarantee of perpetual access. This guarantee is fulfilled via a mandatory End-of-Life (EoL) plan, which requires the publisher to deposit a final, playable version of the product into a designated, official "Permanent Digital Archive." Access and Liability: Access to the archived content must be free of charge for any consumer who can verify their original purchase. Concurrently, the company may sell access keys to new customers, creating a legal route for game preservation and a long-tail revenue stream. The obligation to maintain a verification system for original owners shall persist for as long as the company or any successor entity exists. Upon depositing the product into the archive, the company is absolved of further liability for support or moderation of the product, providing "legal certainty for businesses". Pillar 2: The "Dual Availability" Rule for Digital Content Libraries This pillar addresses loophole concerns related to subscription library services and is designed to prevent "market fragmentation". The Proposed Rule: For a discrete digital product to be included in a subscription library, the rights holder must also make it available for separate, permanent purchase under the terms of Pillar 1, following a reasonable, time-limited exclusivity window (e.g., 12 months). Justification and Impact: This preserves the subscription model while guaranteeing a future path to ownership. It balances exclusivity-driven business strategies with long-term consumer access, preventing a "lack of fairness for consumers" while encouraging platforms to innovate to stay competitive rather than relying on permanent exclusive content. Pillar 3: The "Single Service" Rule for Standalone Subscriptions This pillar addresses long-term, single-product subscription services to protect consumers, "especially where consumer vulnerabilities are exploited for commercial purposes". The Proposed Rule: For a standalone digital service sustained by a subscription model, if the provider decides to permanently discontinue the entire service, they must execute a fair and reasonable End-of-Life (EoL) plan. This plan must include depositing a final, playable version of the service (where technically feasible) into the Permanent Digital Archive, subject to the same access rules defined in Pillar 1. Justification and Impact: This rule recognizes the unique "live service" model while protecting the significant investment of time and money from consumers, including "vulnerable consumers such as minors". It prevents the total loss of this investment, addressing a key area where consumer law is not "adapted to fully address the specific harmful practices and challenges that consumers face online". Conclusion and Implementation This three-pillar, archive-based framework provides a complete and robust solution that will "reduce consumer harm and increase consumer trust". To allow the industry to adapt and minimize "compliance costs", this framework should primarily apply to products released after the Act's implementation. By applying these rules to future products, this approach provides a clear and predictable path forward, encouraging innovation within fair and transparent boundaries and fulfilling the core mission of the Digital Fairness Act.
